payload.claim_text: The Central Business District Tolling Program (CBDTP) aims to reduce congestion in Manhattan's Central Business District and generate sufficient net revenue to support $15 billion for the MTA 2020-2024 Capital Program and subsequent capital programs.
payload.subject: Central Business District Tolling Program

payload.raw_value_text: $1.95 billion in savings over the Plan period
Farebox Revenue Loss Provision of $100 million for 2024

tool_args.label: CBDTP Net Revenue Reduced to $400M for 2024

payload.raw_value_text: Reduce the 2024 projection of the Central Business District Tolling Program's net revenue from $600 million to $400 million
10 percent point increase in paid ridership recovery by 2026

payload.claim_text: A fare-free bus pilot program on five bus routes, lasting six to twelve months, was also authorized.
payload.subject: MTA fare-free bus pilot

payload.claim_text: MTA agreed to invest approximately $35 million annually to improve subway service, and another $35 million in annual investments towards safety and fare enforcement actions.

payload.claim_text: Aligned with these operational efficiencies are the bus lane violation revenues and unscheduled bus operation overtime savings related to the Automated Bus Lane Enforcement (ABLE) measures through camera installation on additional buses and expanded enforcement, as permitted through the State 2023-24 Enacted Budget ($80 million).

payload.gap_text: While the Plan presents balanced budgets through 2027 based on sound budgeting and assumptions, there are risks, including: Continued paid ridership recovery... MTA operating efficiencies... Dedicated tax receipts... Casino revenue... Central Business District Tolling Program
payload.description: The November Plan identifies several risks including ridership recovery, operating efficiency targets, economic slowdown, casino revenue uncertainty ($500 million assumed in 2026 and 2027), and CBDTP/Congestion Pricing litigation risk.
